perturbation.original_text: As used in the Agreement the term "Confidential Information" shall mean any information or data whether of financial, commercial, technical, legal or whatever nature disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party under this Agreement, whether in writing or drawings, orally, in the form of samples, models, computer program or in any form whatsoever including information derivable by visual inspection, provided that such written information is clearly and conspicuously marked as proprietary or confidential and that such oral or visual information is designated as proprietary or confidential upon disclosure and confirmed by the Disclosing Party in writing within thirty (30) days from the date of disclosure, provided that such information shall be treated as Confidential Information by the Receiving Party during this thirty (30) day period.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in the Agreement the term "Confidential Information" shall mean any information or data whether of financial, commercial, technical, legal or whatever nature disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party under this Agreement, whether in writing or drawings, orally, in the form of samples, models, computer program or in any form whatsoever including information derivable by visual inspection, provided that such written information is clearly and conspicuously marked as proprietary or confidential and that such oral or visual information is designated as proprietary or confidential upon disclosure and confirmed by the Disclosing Party in writing within ninety (90) days from the date of disclosure, provided that such information shall be treated as Confidential Information by the Receiving Party during this ninety (90) day period.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the confirmation period for oral disclosures to 90 days. This alteration introduces ambiguity regarding the reasonable timeframe for confirming confidentiality, deviating from standard practices and potentially clashing with trade secret laws that often emphasize prompt action to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Extending the confirmation period to 90 days might be deemed unreasonable in certain industries, thereby jeopardizing trade secret protection. The law emphasizes prompt action, and a prolonged confirmation period could be viewed as a failure to act reasonably to protect the confidentiality of the disclosed information.

perturbation.justification: The modification extends the confirmation period for oral disclosures to 90 days, potentially conflicting with the requirement to take reasonable steps to maintain secrecy under trade secret law. The Law Explanation states that 'UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Extending the confirmation period to 90 days might be deemed unreasonable in certain industries, thereby jeopardizing trade secret protection.' The modification weakens the obligation to promptly protect confidential information, as emphasized by the Law Explanation, and the Perturbation Explanation notes this deviates from standard practices.

perturbation.original_text:
The Receiving Party undertakes during the term of this Agreement and for a period of five (5) years after the date of its expiry or termination:
- to safeguard Confidential Information as it does for its own proprietary information of like importance, but at least with a reasonable degree of care,
- to use Confidential Information only for the Purpose and to divulge Confidential Information to its personnel for said Purpose only and on a "need to know" basis,
- not to duplicate or otherwise reproduce Confidential Information except for such copies as the Receiving Party may require for the Purpose as aforesaid, provided that all copies shall contain the same proprietary and confidential notices and legends as appear on the original Confidential Information,
- not to divulge Confidential Information to any third party for any purpose, unless and until expressly authorized in writing to do so by the Disclosing Party,
- not to reverse-compile, reverse-assemble or reverse-engineer Confidential Information or any part thereof, unless directed to do so by the disclosing Party as proven by appropriate documentation
- not to claim nor to register any Intellectual Property right, nor to exercise any Intellectual Property right or any other right on Confidential Information received under the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text:
The Receiving Party undertakes during the term of this Agreement and for a period of one (1) year after the date of its expiry or termination:
- to safeguard Confidential Information as it does for its own proprietary information of like importance, but at least with a reasonable degree of care,
- to use Confidential Information only for the Purpose and to divulge Confidential Information to its personnel for said Purpose only and on a "need to know" basis,
- not to duplicate or otherwise reproduce Confidential Information except for such copies as the Receiving Party may require for the Purpose as aforesaid, provided that all copies shall contain the same proprietary and confidential notices and legends as appear on the original Confidential Information,
- not to divulge Confidential Information to any third party for any purpose, unless and until expressly authorized in writing to do so by the Disclosing Party,
- not to reverse-compile, reverse-assemble or reverse-engineer Confidential Information or any part thereof, unless directed to do so by the disclosing Party as proven by appropriate documentation
- not to claim nor to register any Intellectual Property right, nor to exercise any Intellectual Property right or any other right on Confidential Information received under the Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period from five years to one year significantly diminishes the protection afforded to the Disclosing Party's confidential information. This may contradict established norms regarding the lifespan of trade secrets, which often extends beyond a single year, especially for complex technologies or research data.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EEA defines trade secrets and criminalizes their misappropriation. A one-year confidentiality period might be deemed insufficient to protect information that constitutes a trade secret under the EEA, especially if the information retains economic value for a longer duration. This reduced period could weaken the Disclosing Party's ability to seek legal recourse under the EEA in case of misappropriation.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The change from a five-year confidentiality period to a one-year period creates a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this reduction may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, particularly under the EEA, where the value of information often extends beyond a year. While the provided law snippet (18 USC 1839) defines terms like 'foreign instrumentality,' it doesn't explicitly state a minimum duration for trade secret protection. However, the EEA's implicit expectation of prolonged value aligns with the argument that a one-year period is inadequate, weakening potential legal recourse for misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: The Agreement is personal to the Parties (« intuitu personae ») and the Receiving Party undertakes not to assign nor transfer its rights or obligations under the Agreement to any third party, including an affiliated company, without the Disclosing Party’s prior written approval.
perturbation.changed_text: The Agreement is personal to the Parties (« intuitu personae ») and the Receiving Party undertakes not to assign nor transfer its rights or obligations under the Agreement to any third party, including an affiliated company, without notifying the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The change replaces 'prior written approval' with 'notifying'. This fundamentally alters the control the Disclosing Party has over assignment, potentially conflicting with legal precedents regarding contract assignments and the protection of confidential information. This conflicts with standard contract law regarding assignment clauses, especially considering confidential information is involved.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 317 generally permits the assignment of rights unless the assignment would materially change the duty of the obligor, increase the burden or risk imposed on him by his contract, impair his chance of obtaining return performance, or reduce its value to him. The original clause prevents the receiving party from assigning any rights without written approval of the disclosing party. The modification would allow for assignment as long as they provide notification, which weakens the protection of the disclosing party.

perturbation.justification: The original contract requires 'prior written approval' for assignment, giving the Disclosing Party control. The changed text replaces this with mere 'notification,' significantly weakening that control. According to the Law Explanation, Section 317 generally permits the assignment of rights unless the assignment would materially change the duty of the obligor, increase the burden or risk imposed on him by his contract, impair his chance of obtaining return performance, or reduce its value to him. Replacing 'prior written approval' with 'notification' materially changes the duty of the obligor and reduces its value to him. While the scraped law snippets are not directly applicable, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation provide a strong basis for concluding that the modification contradicts standard contract law and weakens the protection of the disclosing party, especially considering confidential information is involved, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any dispute arising in connection with this Agreement, including any dispute related to the validity, interpretation, implementation and/or termination of the Agreement, which cannot be amicably settled within the sixty (60) days following its occurrence shall be finally settled under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce by one arbitrator appointed in accordance with those Rules. The arbitration proceedings shall take place in Paris (France). The language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be English. NDA – EUNCL & Sponsor 5/6 The applicable law shall be Belgian Law, under which the dispute, controversy or claim referred to arbitration shall be decided without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute arising in connection with this Agreement, including any dispute related to the validity, interpretation, implementation and/or termination of the Agreement, which cannot be amicably settled within the sixty (60) days following its occurrence shall be finally settled under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce by one arbitrator appointed in accordance with those Rules. The arbitration proceedings shall take place in Paris (France). The language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be English. NDA – EUNCL & Sponsor 5/6 The applicable law shall be Belgian Law, under which the dispute, controversy or claim referred to arbitration shall be decided with regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes 'without regard to its conflict of laws principles' to 'with regard to its conflict of laws principles'. This seemingly small change can have substantial legal implications. By applying conflict of laws principles, the arbitrator must determine which jurisdiction's laws should govern the dispute, which could lead to the application of laws other than Belgian law. This directly contradicts the intent of specifying Belgian law as the governing law.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the FAA primarily concerns the enforceability of arbitration agreements, it also implies a respect for the parties' contractual choices regarding governing law. Modifying the contract to consider conflict of laws principles undermines the initial choice of Belgian law, potentially creating uncertainty and disputes over which law applies. If the dispute were to be arbitrated in the US, this change could lead a US court to question the validity of the choice of law provision, hindering the goals of the FAA to enforce private agreements.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specified that Belgian law would govern the dispute "without regard to its conflict of laws principles." The changed contract states that Belgian law will govern disputes "with regard to its conflict of laws principles." This directly contradicts the original intent. By considering conflict of laws principles, the arbitrator may need to determine which jurisdiction’s laws should govern the dispute, potentially leading to the application of laws other than Belgian law and thus undermining the certainty of the initial choice of law. While the provided snippets from the U.S. Code (9 USC 2) focus on the enforceability of arbitration agreements, the Law Explanation clarifies the underlying principle that parties' contractual choices regarding governing law should be respected, and altering this choice introduces uncertainty.

perturbation.original_text: The Confidential Information is disclosed, “as is”. The Disclosing Party makes no representations or warranties, whether express or implied, with respect to Confidential Information, and notably their fitness for a commercial technical purpose. The use of Confidential Information is made at the sole risk of the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Confidential Information is disclosed, “as is”. The Disclosing Party makes no representations or warranties, whether express or implied, with respect to Confidential Information, and notably their merchantability. The use of Confidential Information is made at the sole risk of the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes 'fitness for a commercial technical purpose' to 'merchantability'. While seemingly similar, 'merchantability' has a very specific legal meaning under the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC). Excluding a warranty of merchantability for information, which is generally not considered 'goods' under the UCC, introduces confusion and a potential legal contradiction. Furthermore, it blurs the line as to whether the information is intended as goods.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC 2-314 implies a warranty of merchantability in contracts for the sale of goods. By disclaiming 'merchantability' in the context of confidential information, the contract implies that the information is being treated as 'goods,' even though information typically is not. This creates a legal ambiguity, as the UCC may not apply to the transfer of confidential information, thus the disclaimer could be deemed ineffective or misleading.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces "fitness for a commercial technical purpose" with "merchantability". The Law Explanation states that disclaiming 'merchantability' in the context of confidential information implies the information is being treated as 'goods', creating a legal ambiguity. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC 2-314, states a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Because information is generally not considered 'goods' under the UCC, the modified contract introduces language that conflicts with the UCC's scope, creating a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.
